turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We will hear argument next in Case No. 14簫449. On the severance question, Mr. McAllister.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The joint sentencing proceeding in these cases did not violate the Eighth Amendment because each defendant received the individualized sentencing determination to which he was entitled. Each presented all of the mitigating evidence he chose to produce. The jury instructions explicitly told the jury to consider each defendant individually. In fact, there were several separate instructions, some that related only to one defendant or only to another defendant. Each 簫簫 the jury for each defendant then had to complete a specific verdict form, and this jury had already proven its ability to distinguish between these two defendants when it convicted one of some counts in the original guilt phase, of which it acquitted the other. The only way the Kansas Supreme Court gets to an Eighth Amendment error in this case 簫簫 well, really, there are two points. One, it relied upon possible, and one instance not even, violations or errors of State law to find a prejudice that 簫簫 that rose to an Eighth Amendment level. And two, it disregarded altogether, with really no explanation, the long簫standing foundational principle that juries are presumed to follow their instructions. The two State law errors, arguably, that 簫簫 the Kansas Supreme Court found, one was the admission of evidence of the sister who made the comment about Reginald may have told me he shot those people. Well, that's not even an error of State law. They said basically that's prejudicial. It might not have come in if they had been tried separately, but in fact, that evidence would be relevant to aggravator number 1, that the defendant killed multiple people. That would have been admissible evidence and that 簫簫 that just can't be an Eighth Amendment violation.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But the Kansas 簫簫 the Kansas Court found independent of the severance question that there was constitutional error in the admission of hearsay.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: That's the third question presented in the cert position on which the Court has not taken any action yet, Your Honor.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Yes. But when it goes back to Kansas, they can still say thank you for telling us about severance, but we have this other ground that leads to the same bottom line.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Well, and we are certainly hopeful that the Court will take another look at that second question depending on its resolution of the two questions that are in front of it today.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: But would 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: But if it goes back in that posture, yes, they would have another ground.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Was 簫簫 was any evidence introduced with respect to one of the defendants that could not have been introduced against the other had there been severance?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: The only arguable evidence really, Justice Kennedy, is what 簫簫 what Reginald refers to as the corrupting influence. But even that, arguably, would be relevant to the mercy consideration to show his character, the nature of this person that is being sentenced. And that was the notion that his mother testified that sometimes 簫簫 of course, Jonathan looked up to his big brother, and maybe sometimes Reginald was a bad influence on him. But that's 簫簫 that was really the extent of that evidence. And that might have come in as a rebuttal to a mercy argument. The Kansas Supreme Court did not really definitively say that was even State law error, but even if you assumed it was 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Could you tell me 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: 簫簫 it would only be State law.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 what the constitutional standard you're asking us to apply? I 簫簫 this argument goes to harmless error; a serious position to take, that even if there was error, no foul 簫簫 a foul but no penalty. Okay? But what standard are we applying? We are not applying the rule 簫簫 or are we or should we apply the Rule 14 standard?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: No, not 簫簫 not for Eighth Amendment purposes, Your Honor. Kansas would propose 簫簫 and I suspect the United States can propose something as well, but it's a very high standard as an Eighth Amendment matter. And we would say 簫簫 we would be content to accept something like it's a serious risk that a defendant will not be able to receive individualized sentencing. It is sort of molding the traditional notions of joinder with the Eighth Amendment consideration, because that seems to be the only Eighth Amendment consideration here, is the individualized sentencing.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Is it the Eighth Amendment or is it due process?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Well, you could use a due process standard, Justice Scalia, which we would also be fine with. The question would really be, did joinder so infect the process with unfairness that the proceedings are fundamentally unfair.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That seems like a 簫簫 a language that is relevant rather than the cruel and unusual punishments language.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Well, and that's exactly what this Court said in Romano v. Oklahoma, a case in which the defendant's other death sentence in a separate case was admitted against the defendant in the sentencing proceeding. That sentence later got reversed, and the Oklahoma court said the jury should not have heard about his other sentence as a matter of State law. That defendant Romano said it is an Eighth Amendment violation, and it's a due process violation. The Court said the Eighth Amendment doesn't have anything to say about what's admissible really. That is State evidentiary rules, that's not an Eighth Amendment issue. And the Court went on to address the due process claim in Romano, applying that very standard. And as we suggest the Court should do here, one of the things it pointed to specifically was jury instructions that said these are the aggravating circumstances you may consider, you may not consider any others. The court said we presume the jury follows its instructions, and nothing in the instructions gave the jury here any way to give effect to that improperly admitted evidence. And that's true here to the extent Reginald Carr argues some of this evidence was really used as a nonstatutory aggravating circumstance. By statute, Kansas does not allow anything other than statutory aggravating circumstances. In the instructions 簫簫 No. 5 I think for Reginald, No. 7 for Jonathan 簫簫 very explicitly and clearly say, here are the four aggravating circumstances the State has alleged. And then the next paragraph, you may not consider anything else as an aggravating circumstance. So in our view, the most the Kansas Supreme Court could come up with is potential violation of State law if, in fact, the evidence which is minimal in the greater scheme of this proceeding that Reginald was a corrupting influence on Jonathan, if that would not have been admitted, that's really all the Kansas Supreme Court is left with to hang its hat on in finding 簫簫 in saying there is an Eighth Amendment violation here.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Can you tell me if you're aware, in other States or perhaps even in Kansas in other trials, is it a common practice to sever when there 簫簫 for the penalty phase when there are multiple defendants?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Justice Kennedy, I think in the briefs 簫簫 I do not remember the exact page 簫簫 there are two States that mandate severance 簫簫 one, Ohio 簫簫 and that's, I think, Georgia and Mississippi.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: This is just for penalty phase?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: I think it's for the entire proceeding. And then Ohio, there is a presumption in the favor of severance, but all the other States, there is nothing that mandates or even, I think, creates a presumption of 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: It seems it would be very difficult for the trial judge to decide which should go first.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: It would, and that's one of the reasons 簫簫 I mean, not just judicial economy but, in a sense, overall fairness 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: 簫簫 having them tried together and you don't have one defendant having an opportunity to preview the evidence that the State may present and the arguments that may be made. This Court has recognized that potential tactical advantage in other joinder cases. Of course, it has not had a joinder capital case like this, but in other circumstances that's a factor. The Court has also emphasized the consistency of determinations with respect to facts. We are not saying the outcomes have to be the same, but when the evidence, the mitigation evidence is 99 percent overlapping 簫簫 they were using the same witnesses, even their experts were co簫authors. They were testifying to the exact same things essentially about each brother. All that overlapping evidence, what this allows is a consistent determination by the jury and evaluation of all that mitigating evidence that leads to whatever outcome the jury decides. But the jury was very clearly told to consider each defendant individually. And that's what they got, was individual consideration. I would say also, remember, even if this Court were to think there were some kind of error which Kansas simply does not see here 簫簫 again, at most, possibly an error of Kansas law, not an Eighth Amendment violation 簫簫 it would have to be harmless under any standard, and Kansas would accept any standard that is beyond a reasonable doubt certainly here. Given the four uncontested aggravating circumstances 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, you'd also have to look 簫簫 you have it at the top of your head, maybe forget if you don't, but what I thought I would do is I wanted to look to the aggravating circumstances, what were they.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: What were they.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Let's call it "he was the monster" approach. Reginald was the monster and Jonathan was the puppet or whatever. I want to look at the aggravators. I also want to look at the mitigators that Reginald said existed, and then see if this comparative monster, let's call it, fits into any of them. Do you know? Do you know the pages where they are, just by chance?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Well, I think the 簫簫 in terms of the 簫簫 yes, the aggravating circumstances are set forth in the instructions, Justice Breyer, so 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Okay. I know where they are.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Yeah, Instruction No. 5 for Reginald and Instruction No. 7 for Jonathan, and then following each one, so I believe Instruction No. 6 and Instruction No. 8. But the mitigating 簫簫 those are the statutory mitigating circumstances. So what happens in these cases is the jury is told, here are the statutory. And we list all of them that are listed in the Kansas statute. And then you can 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: On what page do you list them?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Well, so those are listed in Instruction No. 6 and Instruction No. 8. It's all in the petition 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: If, in fact, this gets in, then I guess what I have to do is go through this huge record on the sentencing anyway and see do I really think that this evidence that came in that the younger one thought the older one was the monster, et cetera, did it really have an effect?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Is that what I would have to do? I don't see how I could avoid that if they are at all relevant.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Well, I would say, first of all, if were you to do that, it would be quite clear. The evidence is overwhelming. But furthermore, the premise is not supported really by the record. They say Jonathan's whole strategy was to paint Reginald as the bad actor here. That's not really what happened. What they both presented was lots of witnesses and testimony about their 簫簫 their childhood and their experiences growing up, and they presented psychological experts to talk about their mental condition. No expert said Reginald had some kind of psychological control over Jonathan. That 簫簫 that testimony, to the extent it's there at all, comes from their mother.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But what do you think about a case, whether or not this is that case, in which it is quite clear to a judge that each of two defendants is just going to be pointing to the other person and saying, that was the bad actor. He is why these terrible crimes committed. And you know that 簫簫 you know, each of them is going to be doing that. Should a judge separate the penalty proceeding in that context?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Well, not necessarily. If the high standard is met that it will not be possible, perhaps, to give individualized consideration to each in an Eighth Amendment or a due process standard is going to be so fundamentally unfair, if you can meet that very high standard, then 簫簫 but that's always been the Court's approach. It is a case簫by簫case determination. Even Zafiro, which is a joinder case 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, I guess I am asking you in what kind of factual circumstances you would find that standard met?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Rarely, but I think it would be 簫簫 certainly the Court has recognized in Bruton, you have some sort of co簫defendant confession 簫簫 I mean, confession situations, if there was something that had not been used in the guilt phase, might be relevant in the sentencing. That might be so prejudicial. We have tried to think of other examples. I might suggest something like one is claiming at that point in time some kind of insanity, even though they didn't succeed on that at trial. If there was something really starkly contradictory in their presentations and you were going to get a lot of evidence about one that really had nothing to do at all with the other. But we do believe it's a high standard that would rarely be satisfied, especially given the Court's long簫standing presumption that the jury follows its instructions. And that's one thing I would point out here, the complaints that each makes in this Court. First of all, at least one of those complaints really wasn't even made in the Kansas Supreme Court, much less the trial court, and that's the shackling of Reginald and whether that might have prejudiced Jonathan. But they 簫簫 there was no complaints during the sentencing proceeding. This corrupting influence evidence is relevant only for Jonathan's mitigation case, not with respect to Reginald; can we have a limiting instruction? Reginald has anti簫social personality disorder; there was no request for any sort of limiting that that 簫簫 that's not applicable to Jonathan. Although, of course, the irony there is that Jonathan's own expert all but diagnosed him as anti簫social personality. He said out of 26 risk factors that the Department of Justice has recognized for violence and sexual violence, Jonathan has 24 of them. And both brothers talked about family history of mental illness. Both brothers and their experts talked about whether some of these things were hereditary or genetic. This was really a case in which it made very good sense to proceed with a joint proceeding, because the evidence, 99 percent was overlapping, essentially. I mean, they were calling the same witnesses, taking turns with those witnesses. Judicial economy nor fairness, neither one would have been served any better by separating this into two proceedings. And, in fact, I will conclude for now, unless there are further questions, with this observation, that prior to trial, to the guilt phase, the prosecution actually offered to have two juries sit through this proceeding. Jonathan's attorney said he would consider it, and Reginald's attorney rejected it. So the State even tried to 簫簫 to agree to some workable system and 簫簫 and it was declined. So unless there are further questions, I will reserve the remainder of my time.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Ms. Kovner. SUPPORTING PETITIONER

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The Kansas Supreme Court erred when it found that the State violated the Eighth Amendment by conducting joint sentencing proceedings for the crimes the Respondents committed together. Joint proceedings can enhance accuracy and fairness, as a long line of this Court's cases hold. They provide a fuller evidentiary record to juries that are assessing relative culpability, and they prevent arbitrary disparities that may arise when two juries reach inconsistent conclusions about the common facts of a single crime.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Ms. Kovner, is there any difference between the Federal government's position and the position of the State of Kansas on this question?

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: As to the standard, I think there's a small difference. I think our proposal is that the constitutional standard is whether evidence or argument resulted in a denial of due process or the deprivation of an individualized sentencing proceeding. And that's slightly different from Kansas's standard, because Kansas is proposing a rule that involves analysis of risk, and we think that is part of the statutory rule under Rule 14, as Justice Sotomayor alluded to. It's also part of the statutory standard that states "by and large are applying," but we think the constitutional question is just, was this person deprived of a fair trial by the evidence that came in? And that's the constitutional question.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: When you said it the first time, you had "deprived of a fair trial" and then something about individualized sentencing; is that right? Is that supposed to indicate that this is deriving both from the Due Process Clause and from the Eighth Amendment?

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: Well, the Eighth Amendment does speak to this need for an individualized sentencing determination. We would think that the Due Process Clause also requires that proceedings be individualized. But we would agree that if there were a situation where there was a joint trial and because of the evidence introduced by one, for instance, a jury simply could not give individualized consideration to a second 簫簫 second defendant, that would be a constitutional problem. And to give an example of that 簫簫 I mean, I think the example the Court suggests in Zafiro of that is where there is aggravating evidence that pertains only to one defendant, but that that aggravating evidence is just so prejudicial and so overpowering that the jury couldn't really separate the two and would be inclined to sort of judge the second one by association. So I think that's the example the Court gives in Zafiro. But it's not 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Don't you think individualized consideration is required even in the non簫capital cases?

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: Absolutely, Your Honor. So I think 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It's really a due process consideration, isn't it?

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: I think due process encompasses that requirement of individualized consideration. Just to address several of the points that came up during Petitioner's argument, I think 簫簫 as to the question that Justice Kagan asked about whether, when defendants are simply pointing the finger at each other, that would necessitate severance. I think that's the question that the Court addressed in Zafiro, where that was essentially the claim the defendants were making, that we're both going to point the finger at each other at trial. And the Court said that doesn't necessitate severance; that that may, in fact, increase the jury's ability to reach an accurate verdict, to reach an accurate assessment of relative culpability, and enhance fairness. So we don't think that that's a situation that would require severance.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I 簫簫 I guess the strongest evidence for Reginald is Temica's testimony. It's 簫簫 it's pretty prejudicial. She said he's 簫簫 he's the one who 簫簫 he said he's the one who shot.

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: I don't think that that's prejudicial evidence, certainly not any constitutional problem with the admission of that evidence. I don't think that even Reginald Carr is asserting that that evidence couldn't have come in against him. Rather he is asserting or the State below suggested 簫簫 the State court below suggested maybe that 簫簫 maybe the State didn't know about that evidence, and maybe just as a factual matter it wouldn't have come in. But there's no constitutional unfairness with evidence coming in on a co簫defendant's case that's harmful but accurate as to you. That's something the Court said in 簫簫 in Zafiro. If it's accurate and relevant evidence that comes in on a co簫defendant's case, there's no constitutional unfairness as to you. I think Reginald's primary claim before this Court is that there was a violation of State evidentiary law because some of the other evidence, the evidence of bad influence, might not have come in under the State rules. I think this Court's cases are clear that a violation of State evidentiary law is simply a matter of State law. The State could decide that necessitates reversal, but it's not a constitutional error. That's what this Court said in Romano.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It would depend on what is was, wouldn't it? I mean, so I'm still asking the same question. If the 簫簫 I take it that Reginald could not have introduced evidence refuting these brothers' state of mind that he was the monster. That's what you're referring to by the bad influence. So the jury might have taken that evidence into account in trying to decide whether Reginald, for other reasons than in the statute, was a sympathetic enough character not to provide the death penalty. That's conceivable. But severance is very, very rare, and joint trials are very common. And very common is the claim on an appeal in any kind of a case that it shouldn't 簫簫 it should have been severed. So do you have cases that you can think of that would be good precedent for you where a court, including particularly this Court, said, of course there might be a little prejudice here, some, but it's not enough to warrant severance? Because I think what I have to do is read the record on this point, and then I need something to compare it with.

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: Well, I want to go back 簫簫 I'd like to answer the question and then go back to the premise 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: 簫簫 because I am not sure I agree with the premise. But as to whether any prejudice is a problem, I think the best case for us is Zafiro. Zafiro talks about the idea that what you need 簫簫 that joinder is presumably going to enhance fairness and accuracy. And what you need is substantial prejudice, even to have a statutory problem, and then 簫簫 even then we are going to presume that a limiting instruction would be sufficient to cure it. So that would be our best case on that point. But, Your Honor, I think the inquiry under the Eighth Amendment has to be would the Constitution have prohibited this evidence from coming in as to Reginald Carr. And we think the answer is no. Any evidence that's relevant to an individual defendant 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, normally in severance cases, forgetting the death cases, seems to me the argument has been, look, they never would have gotten this piece of evidence in against my client. They brought it in against the other client. Now, go look at that evidence and you'll see how much my prejudice 簫簫 that my client was prejudiced by that. I do not know whether you call that due process. Maybe you do. But that's the kind of argument which seems for a quantitative weighing. That's why I asked the question.

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: I think I would go back to Zafiro again on that one, Your Honor, because I think Zafiro establishes that when accurate evidence comes in through a co簫defendant that is relative 簫簫 that's relevant to the jury's determination, that's not prejudice. That's information that may enhance the accuracy of what the jury 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Can you tell me why we should apply the Zafiro standard, which was joint trials, but this is joint sentencing where there is a different 簫簫 why there should be exactly the same standard applied?

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: So we think Zafiro 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Do you need individualized sentencing? So can't you say, or aren't you required to say that something a little bit more than 簫簫 than the efficiency of a joint trial has to compel 簫簫

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 has to be considered?

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. We agree that sentencings may involve special considerations. What we think Zafiro establishes is that generally when more relevant information is placed before a jury, that's not prejudice to a defendant, that's not unfair, and that juries with more information are likely to make more accurate decisions, and we think that's equally true at sentencing. And, similarly, that juries with more 簫簫 who are confronted with two defendants together can avoid the unwarranted disparities that may occur when juries reach different results 簫簫 two different juries considering the same facts reach different results on those facts. And that's equally true at sentencing.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Sorry, but I'm not 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You 簫簫 you would need two 簫簫 two separate juries, wouldn't you? I mean 簫簫

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: That's 簫簫 that's what the Kansas Supreme Court said here. And the result of that is 簫簫 is going to be that two different juries confronted with the very same aggravating circumstances in largely parallel mitigation cases may simply weigh factors like mercy differently.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But wouldn't 簫簫 wouldn't the second jury have to know the facts relevant to mitigation, as well as aggravation, I suppose, that came out in the main trial?

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: It's 簫簫 it's certainly possible, Your Honor, that if there was a second jury, the government could simply introduce, for instance, the evidence of Reginald Carr's statement to his sister at the second trial. So you're introducing this disparity that Justice Kennedy alluded to between the defendant who goes first and the defendant who goes second.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, would this second jury have been present for the 簫簫 for the guilt phase?

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: Well, in 簫簫 in the Federal system, there are a number of ways you could do it. Could you have two juries that hear both the guilt phase and 簫簫 and the penalty phase. You could have a second jury impanelled only for the 簫簫 the penalty phase. But then in a case like this, you would need to essentially repeat all the trial evidence, including calling victims again, because that 簫簫 because the State was relying on its evidence from the trial phase, and that is generally, we submit, going to be the case.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I think I missed something you said, but 簫簫 forgive me. But the constitutional standard that you're proposing, is that a constitutional standard for both the guilt phase and the sentencing phase? And is it also for both non簫capital and capital? Are you drawing no distinctions among those four things?

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: I think that's right, Your Honor. I mean, that 簫簫 because we think that the due process standard essentially subsumes the requirement of individualized consideration, and, of course, due process is applicable at the trial phase and at the sentencing phase in capital and non簫capital trials.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So it seems a little bit counterintuitive to me, the idea that, you know, the guilt phase in a very minor crime would have the exact same standard applicable to it as the guilt phase of a capital crime and then as the sentencing phase of a capital crime.

turns.name: RACHEL P. KOVNER
turns.text: If I may answer really briefly? Your Honor, we think the reason that's the case is that joint trials often enhance accuracy and fairness. So we agree that accuracy and fairness considerations are at their paramount in capital cases, but we do not think that militates for a different standard in capital cases because we think that the standard that the courts are applying is one that's going to generally enhance those values.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Liu.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may please the Court: I want to begin by emphasizing how narrow our rule is. Justice Kagan asked the question involving a 簫簫 a case where the two defendants pointed their fingers at each other. We are not advocating a rule that would require severance in any case where two defendants point their fingers at 簫簫 at each other. We are advocating a rule that this Court has recognized in Zant, Stringer, in Sanders, that 簫簫 that an Eighth Amendment violation occurs when the weighing process itself is skewed. And the weighing 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But are you saying the severance rule is the same in non簫capital as in capital cases?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: The general severance rule, Your Honor, is the same.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And you just look for prejudice?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Exactly. The general rule is you can't have severance when it would compromise someone's constitutional rights. The right at issue here just happens to be an Eighth Amendment right, but we are not advocating an Eighth Amendment rule that would apply beyond the capital context or even beyond the penalty phase. That's 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You know, if you violated an individualized sentence, why would we apply harmless error review? Meaning, it seems counterintuitive. We are now becoming the sentencing 簫簫 the sentencing body. No?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Absolutely. And I think 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Oh, so then we have to do the 簫簫 in harmless error, we have to decide whether the aggravators outweighed the mitigators, et cetera, so much that none of the error could have affected that choice?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Well, this Court, Your Honor, recognized in Satterwhite precisely how difficult it would be to conduct a harmless error analysis in a penalty phase, and that's precisely because there are so many factors involved. Sure, the crimes in the case were horrific, but that's just one side of the scale in a harmless error inquiry. There is an entire other side of the balance, the mitigation side. And so it is quite difficult for this Court, on a cold record, to go through the harmless error analysis.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I 簫簫 I 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: We have to do it, though, right? I mean, that's 簫簫 that's part of 簫簫 part of our jurisprudence. So what 簫簫 what do you think are the 簫簫 are 簫簫 are the factors that would suggest the jury would have come out a different way had the rule that you urge been adopted? What 簫簫 what specifically? One is the shackling of the 簫簫 of the co簫defendant?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Well, we are not representing Reginald Carr. We're not basing our claim around the shackling. Our 簫簫 our claim revolves around the evidence that Jonathan presented that Reginald had a corrupting influence on him while they were growing up. And that evidence, as the Kansas Supreme Court itself held at Petition Appendix 411, falls beyond the rubric of any valid sentencing factor.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But, Mr. Liu, I mean, given the kind of evidence that was presented in this case, the idea that somebody was a lousy big brother seems pretty small on 簫簫 in the 簫簫 in the scale of things.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Well, a few responses to that. To begin with, I think Your 簫簫 Your Honor is understating the evidence here. This wasn't just that Reginald was a lousy big brother. It was that he did things to Jonathan that turned Jonathan into the person who was capable of committing and even leading these crimes. So this wasn't just "lousy big brother" evidence, this was evidence that Reginald himself was the source of what caused Jonathan to do the things he did. But, Your Honor, I think it's also important to keep in mind two separate parts of the inquiry; there is the violation and the harmless error analysis. And this Court said in Stringer that even a thumb on the scales is enough to skew the weighing process in violation of the Eighth Amendment.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: But what 簫簫 what evidence that shouldn't have been admitted wouldn't have been admitted in the 簫簫 in the separate trial?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Well, all this evidence, Mr. Chief Justice, that says Reginald was a corrupting influence on his brother, that wouldn't have come in in a separate trial for two reasons: Number one, as the Kansas Supreme Court held at Petition Appendix 411, that evidence was improper, nonstatutory, aggravating evidence, which is to say, all the aggravating factors before the prosecution pursuit in this case all had to do with the circumstances of the crime. But this evidence had nothing to do with the circumstances of what happened on December 15th, it had to do with the defendant's character concerning circumstances that occurred years, even decades, before the crimes at issue.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Now, wait a minute. You're 簫簫 you're representing the brother who was the alleged corrupting influence, right?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: That's right, the corruptor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The corruptor was sentenced, by the same jury, to death. So how could it possibly be that without the corrupting action of your client, the jury would have sentenced your client to life even though the corruptee was sentenced to death? That doesn't seem to me at all likely.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Well, to begin 簫簫 begin with, I don't think Jonathan Carr's sentence is a proper baseline to use. As my friend will argue in a few minute, his own sentence was prejudiced by severance. But even moving beyond that, there is every reason to believe that the jury viewed Jonathan as overall more culpable than Reginald. After all, the State never established who the shooter was here. If the jury believed that Jonathan was the shooter, the jury could have believed that Jonathan was so culpable that even with his evidence that he had been corrupted by Reginald, it would have given a life sentence. I think 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Let me 簫簫 let me put the crime to you. You tell me which of these descriptions of the 簫簫 of the crime are 簫簫 are incorrect. These two men broke into a house in which there were three men and two women. They ordered the five to remove their clothes, forced them into a closet. Over the course of three hours, they demanded that the two women perform various sexual acts on one another. They demanded at gunpoint that each of the three men have sexual intercourse with both women. Then Reginald drove the victims one by one to various ATMs to withdraw cash. Jonathan raped or attempted to rape both women twice, and Reginald raped Holly G., who later testified, once. Placing the three men, still naked, in the trunk of one of their cars, the cars drove all five to a soccer field, forced them to kneel in the snow, and shot them execution簫style in the back of the head. One of them, fortuitously, was not killed because 簫簫 I think it was a hair clip that she was wearing deflected the bullet. And she is the one who testified to all of this activity. And you truly think 簫簫 oh, and they ran over her too. After shooting her in the head, the car ran over her. You truly think that this jury, but for the fact that your client was a corruptor, would not have imposed the death penalty?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: We do, Justice Scalia. In your own opinion in last term in Glossip, you noted that the egregiousness of an offense is just one factor in determining whether a sentence is appropriate at the penalty phase. And Reginald Carr submitted a week and a half full of mitigation evidence that extenuated this offense. So I don't 簫簫 I think when you 簫簫 when you take all that all into account and consider the fact that this wasn't an easy case for the jury 簫簫 the jury, after all, deliberated a full day on what the 簫簫 what Kansas thinks is quite an easy case. I think the 簫簫 the scales of the weighing process were much more evenly balanced than that.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Do you think that the desire to spare Holly from having to testify more than once is a relevant factor here? And if so, how could that be 簫簫 how do you 簫簫 how would you propose to accommodate them?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: It's not a relevant factor and that's not my saying it, it's this Court saying it. This Court in Zafiro and Bruton has said that the benefits of joinder, while they may exist, cannot come at the price of constitutional rights. So while I 簫簫 I completely appreciate that severance would require some duplication of resources, that's precisely the sort of benefit that this Court has said cannot trump the Eighth Amendment.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So if you're right, joinder being among the most common kind of thing that gangs and drugs and so forth, why won't the same argument be made over and over preventing 簫簫 requiring severance in dozens and dozens, perhaps hundreds of cases where the government tries people together? Because they will say there are different relationships among members of the gang. Those relationships 簫簫 some evidence would come in that would negative the relationship that would tend to show that this particular individual wasn't actually involved in this aspect of it, et cetera. That's why my experience on the Courts, lower courts particularly, leads me to think it's a very rarely accepted argument, severance.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Well, you're right, Justice Breyer 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And that's why I'm interested in 簫簫 I mean, because if it were, you'd find it very unusual to try people together, which, in fact, is very usual. And so when you told me there is no separate thing for the death cases, then I imagine it would affect every criminal trial of gangs throughout the country.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Well, I think 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And that is something that's concerning me and I am looking for an answer.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: You're absolutely right, Justice Breyer, that under our rule, the circumstances of each case are relevant. But I think what's also important is 簫簫 is that some 簫簫 the circumstances of some cases might not get rise for severance.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That's what I'm looking for. Why is it you believe that if I were to decide in your favor on this case and find the sufficient prejudice to warrant severance here, I wouldn't at the same time be throwing a huge monkey wrench 簫簫

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 into the ordinary cases of gangs, drugs, et cetera, where joinder is very common and reversal is very rare.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Because the evidence here is special in the sense that it fell beyond any existing sentencing factor.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Are you proposing just separate sentencing hearings, or are you proposing separate juries for each of the brothers?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Well, the Kansas Supreme Court in this case ordered as its remedy a new trial with two juries, but I don't think that is necessarily going to be the required remedy in every case. There are different ways, Justice Ginsburg, to solve the problems of sentencing.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Let's take this case. If you had only one jury and Jonathan goes first, that jury, when it gets to Reginald's case, is not going to forget everything it heard in Jonathan's case.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: You're absolutely right, Justice Ginsburg. I don't think a sequential solution with Jonathan going first is going to work in this case precisely because the jury won't 簫簫 you won't be able to unring the bell of the 簫簫 precisely the evidence we think is prejudicial in this case. But that's not to say that a sequential solution isn't going to work in the mine run of cases. This case is special just because there 簫簫 there is some allegation that the prejudice ran both ways.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: But the minute 簫簫 but the minute you defend the idea of two different juries, then you sacrifice the desirability and the possibility of consistency.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Well, Justice Kennedy, there are many ways that States have developed to resolve the problem of inconsistent verdicts among different juries. One way is what the Georgia State has done, which is had proportionality review. They ask the appellate courts to look across many sentences to see if they are inconsistent. Another way the Federal government has a solution to this, is that let one jury know how the other jury sentenced the other defendant. That's 18 U.S.C. 3592(a)(4). And so there are ways to solve the problem of inconsistent verdicts across juries. But what's 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: That sounds 簫簫 that sounds pretty prejudicial to me. If the second case, you say, oh, the jury considered this and individual circumstances, you know, these people act together, and by the way, a jury of your peers unanimously found beyond a reasonable doubt that this guy should be sentenced to death; now do whatever you want with this guy. That sounds pretty prejudicial to me.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Well, it's 簫簫 it's 簫簫 if you think that is prejudicial, Mr. Chief Justice, then this proceeding was quite prejudicial too because the jury was asked to make that same exact comparison. But the specific prejudice we are alleging here is 簫簫 is unique to these or at least quite 簫簫 quite restricted to the circumstances of this case.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Liu, could you, for me, just tell me what is the specific prejudice you're alleging here? In other words, tell me more than just, oh, he was a corrupting influence. What evidence particularly came in 簫簫

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: 簫簫 and why do you think it might have mattered to the jury? Just give me your, like, best shot.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Sure. Well, Kansas said there was no expert testimony on this, but that's wrong. Dr. Cunningham, at Joint Appendix pages 324 to 329, explained how Jonathan looked up to Reggie, and every time they would get together they would do drugs heavily together, that when 簫簫 that when they were ages 6 or 7, Reginald prompted someone to have sex with Jonathan. These are precisely the sort of evidence that shows that however bad you think Jonathan is, Reginald is worse because Reginald is the one who created the person Jonathan is. It's in a sense making Reginald doubly culpable for these offenses; that, well, whatever you think Jonathan did 簫簫 and yes, as Justice Scalia read, there were some horrific things done 簫簫 well, Reginald should be punished for not only everything Reginald did, but also everything Jonathan did. So this is precisely the sort of evidence that basically transfers all of Jonathan's actions to Reginald. And that's why it's so prejudicial when you get in the jury room, it's because it 簫簫 it skews the weighing process. And a skewing of the weighing process is an error this Court has recognized for 30 years. This isn't something that we 簫簫 we came up special for this case. This Court has recognized time and again that when a jury is told to consider an improper element in the weighing process, the weighing process is therefore skewed. And the only thing that can cure that error is either harmless error review or 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But it 簫簫

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: 簫簫 reweighing by the State court.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But it has to have been harmless inasmuch as the person who was influenced by your client also got the death penalty. How can you say that what made the difference was the fact that your client was a corrupting influence upon his younger brother?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Well, Justice Scalia, the State never established the identity of the shooter, and that is the key horrific act in this case. If the jury believed Jonathan was the shooter, then the jury might have given him the death penalty anyway regardless of what Reginald did to him.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I doubt whether that is the key. You really think that that's the only thing the jury is going to be focused on, is who pulled the trigger? My Lord.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: I don't think it's the only 簫簫 I don't think it's the only thing, but it's certainly the main thing given that we are here on a capital murder charge.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Do you think that on a retrial they cannot use the sister to prove that Reginald claimed he was the shooter?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: No. And I think that just highlights exactly how narrow our rule is. That the evidence that Temica said that Reginald told her that Reginald was the shooter goes to the circumstances of the crime. And that sort of finger pointing, who shot who on the day of the crime, is going to fall comfortably within an existing sentencing factor, and as a result, is not going to skew the weighing process.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So the only issue you think was prejudicial was that he was a corruptor?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The evidence.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: And for reasons I 簫簫 I gave to Justice Kagan, that evidence was extremely prejudicial. It skewed the weighing process because it transferred Jonathan's culpability back onto Reginald's shoulders. If I may, I'd just like to address a few points raised by my friend from Kansas. He said the instructions solve the problem. Well, none of the instructions told the jury to consider this evidence about the corrupting influence only as to Jonathan. Now, on the contrary, the instructions 簫簫 Instruction No. 2 told the jury consider the evidence applicable to each defendant. Well, this evidence was equally applicable to Reginald as it was to Jonathan. After all, it was Reginald 簫簫 Reginald's actions that were at issue. My friends also point to the instruction on statutory aggravators. But that disregards an entire swath of evidence that the jury heard that it could consider against Reginald. This was evidence, anti簫mitigation evidence that the jury considered, could have caused them to remove weight from the mitigation side of the scale. And the instructions left the jury completely free to consider this evidence in anti簫mitigation. My friends also suggest that I'm here only arguing an error of State law. Well, no less than the Petitioners were in Zant, Stringer, and 簫簫 and Sanders. Each of those cases recognizes that a skewing of the weighing process has to be based on State law premises. That's why this Court in Zant went so far as to certify a question to the Georgia Supreme Court. Yes, it's true that to understand the effect on the weighing process, you have to look at the State law. But the mere admission of evidence isn't what causes the violation here. It's the admission of the evidence, followed by the fact the jury was required to consider it. And it was required to consider it because it was part and parcel of Jonathan's mitigating evidence. And this Court has said time and again that a defendant has a constitutional right for the jury, not only to consider but to listen to a defendant's mitigating evidence. So by considering this evidence for Jonathan, the jury necessarily considered it against Reginald. After all, if you're told that Reginald had a corrupting influence on Jonathan, you can't separate that as something for Jonathan and something for Reginald. It's equally applicable to both. My friend also suggested that we 簫簫 we rejected a two簫jury solution before trial. Well, we rejected that, not because of it wouldn't work to solve any prejudice in the penalty phase, but because it wouldn't work to solve any prejudice in the guilt phase. And remember, the Kansas Supreme Court found that it was actually error to keep the guilt phase joined, so we were perfectly appropriate in objecting to a two簫jury solution at that phase of the trial.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Can you explain that again? I thought you 簫簫 you sought only a severance at the sentencing phase.

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: No. That's not 簫簫 that's not 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: You sought severance totally?

turns.name: FREDERICK LIU
turns.text: Exactly. Prior to trial, on pages 25 and 26 of the Joint Appendix, we moved for severance of the entire thing. And if you look at those pages, you'll see Jonathan's attorney, Jonathan's own attorney, saying that if the penalty phase is joined, he is going to have to present evidence that, quote, "there's no way the State would be able to introduce if Reginald was sitting here alone." Well, his attorney was exactly right. That's exactly what happened many months later. I see that my time is up.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Green.

turns.name: JEFFREY T. GREEN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I'd like to first try and change the narrative here with some illustrations about the difference between the mitigation cases of both defendants. That's easily seen in the testimony of the experts. Reginald Carr presented evidence from a neuroscientist by the name of Dr. Woltersdorf that said his client had been diagnosed a 簫簫 an incurable sociopath. Jonathan Carr's forensic psychologist, clinical and forensic psychologist, got up and testified that 簫簫 that Jonathan was sensitive, he was affectionate, he had attempted suicide, and what he really was, contrary to what Mr. McAllister represented to you, was 簫簫 was depressed and schizophrenic. That is 簫簫 that is a unique illustration of exactly how different these two defendants were in their presentation, and why, again, a sequential penalty phase, even if they had been separate, wouldn't work. Reginald says he didn't want to hear or he thought it would be prejudicial if the jury heard evidence from the 簫簫 from the 簫簫 from Jonathan's witnesses about Reginald being a 簫簫 a corrupting influence. On the other side, Jonathan doesn't want the jury to hear from Reginald's expert. And to answer your question, Justice Kagan, that's the evidence that would not have been introduced. Justice Woltersdorf's 簫簫 or excuse me 簫簫 Dr. Woltersdorf's testimony about incurable sociopathy would not have been relevant or admissible 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Maybe I'm missing something, but if 簫簫 if your client's strategy was to make Reginald as bad 簫簫 the bad actor in the case, why didn't that evidence actually go hand in hand with your client's strategy, which was to say it's all on Reginald?

turns.name: JEFFREY T. GREEN
turns.text: One 簫簫 one would think that it would in 簫簫 in a typical case, and it may be that some jurors had inferred that way. The problem was that 簫簫 that the fact that the two brothers were sitting together allowed the prosecutors to repeatedly paint them with the same brush. And 簫簫 and if we take a look, for example, at JA 402, the prosecutor says to the jury in her opening argument at the beginning of the penalty 簫簫 or at the conclusion of the penalty phase, ladies and gentlemen, they have the same eye color. They are now wearing glasses, although their mother said Reginald doesn't need them, they share some DNA.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So this 簫簫 maybe you can, in your experience, cure what's bothering me about the case. It's nothing to do with the facts, or for this 簫簫 this concern. It has to do with 簫簫 with Zafiro.

turns.name: JEFFREY T. GREEN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And that's the ordinary case of joint trials, right? And what the Court says is you can have the joinder as long as there isn't a serious risk that the joint trial would compromise 簫簫 it doesn't say harmless error, it says compromise 簫簫 a specific trial right, or prevent the jury from making a reliable judgment. That doesn't talk about harmless error. Now, that's the standard that's used in dozens and dozens and dozens of cases. Or if, in this case, we start talking about harmless error, and that you can't have the joint proceeding if there is error that is not harmless. Well, what have we done to that sentence? And what have we done to the trials that are joint in hundreds of cases that don't involve murder or death? That is the legal problem that is worrying me. And you will either cure that worry or say I've made some elementary mistake. It has nothing to do with your case. Say what you want, but I want to hear what you think about it.

turns.name: JEFFREY T. GREEN
turns.text: I don't 簫簫 I don't think you've made any mistake at all, Justice Breyer. Indeed, I think that 簫簫 that the State of Kansas and the Solicitor General are offering nothing more than 簫簫 than the Zafiro test and using slightly different language. On page 15 of our 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So we should not use the words "harmless error." We should quote from Zafiro and say could they make a reliable judgment? Now, that sounds harder on you, because if all you have to show is the error was harmful, it sounds like an easier task you have than to show that the juror 簫簫 jury wasn't reliable. Or are they the same thing in your opinion?

turns.name: JEFFREY T. GREEN
turns.text: Well, let me 簫簫 I think they're the same thing and I think that's a generalization, but we've 簫簫 we've offered a test that actually specifies it a little bit more, Justice Breyer. In terms of saying that 簫簫 that the test should be whether there is a reasonable risk 簫簫 and I will explain the difference between reasonable and serious in a minute 簫簫 but whether there was a reasonable risk that there would be evidence introduced, material, prejudicial evidence introduced that would 簫簫 that would, in fact, not have been introduced in a severed penalty簫phase proceeding. And to go back to Justice Kagan's question about the intuitive difference between these two things, they're between 簫簫 excuse me 簫簫 trials and 簫簫 and penalty簫phase proceedings, the answer is that's because in a 簫簫 in a trial, the 簫簫 the relative culpability of the defendants makes a big difference with respect to who's a conspirator, who's not a conspirator, who's a principal, who's an aider and abettor. But when we turn to the penalty phase, the inquiry changes, as Mr. Liu indicated 簫簫 it changes from exactly what happened to who this person is and whether or not the jury wants to put this person to death.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Who 簫簫 who 簫簫 if you have separate proceedings, who 簫簫 who gets to go second? Because obviously that person will have a significant advantage since they'll see all of the evidence presented in the 簫簫 in the other proceeding.

turns.name: JEFFREY T. GREEN
turns.text: Well, in 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: And most of the evidence here was overlapping, so that at least you'll have 簫簫 you'll be able to see what the State thought about that evidence. You have sort of a dry run if you're the second person.

turns.name: JEFFREY T. GREEN
turns.text: Well, in this case, respectfully, no, Mr. Chief Justice, because what the 簫簫 what the State basically said was we're relying on our evidence from the 簫簫 from the trial phase 簫簫 or from the guilt phase of this capital trial. That's exactly what the prosecutor told the jurors when they turned in to the penalty phase, and all that the prosecution did in the penalty phase was cross簫examine the defense witnesses.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, but that cross簫examination is important. Were there any common defense witnesses?

turns.name: JEFFREY T. GREEN
turns.text: There were family members. So 簫簫 so it is true 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So at least it would be 簫簫 it would be helpful for the second 簫簫 the person who goes second to know, well, what did the prosecutor talk 簫簫 how did the prosecutor attempt to cross簫examine that witness, because I am going to call the same witness, and her testimony is going to be a lot better the second time around because you will know exactly what the cross簫examination questions are going to be.

turns.name: JEFFREY T. GREEN
turns.text: That might happen. I 簫簫 it 簫簫 it could well be that that would be 簫簫 I would consider that a 簫簫 a minor advantage, Your Honor. But that could be handled by the judge in 簫簫 with protective orders, with orders about 簫簫 rules against witnesses and 簫簫 and folks in the courtroom who will have another proceeding. But I would submit 簫簫 and then with respect to witnesses who might have been traumatized by these events, that 簫簫 the 簫簫 you know, a trial court could easily have a simultaneous penalty proceedings so that that witness would only have to testify once. The defense team on one side wouldn't get an advantage over the other. There would be two juries in two different courtrooms, same week, same days, the 簫簫 the witness can go back and forth between courtrooms, and there is none of the advantage 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: You would have 簫簫 the State would have to put on, basically, its guilt phase case again at the penalty phase, right?

turns.name: JEFFREY T. GREEN
turns.text: Yes, it would have to do that, but I would imagine that 簫簫 that with respect to cases like this, the defense attorneys are going to move right into what counts as stipulations; we will read testimony 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, I am sure you'd like to stipulate to all the facts that were proven at the guilt phase, but I doubt that the State is going to want to stipulate to those.

turns.name: JEFFREY T. GREEN
turns.text: And the State might do that. And again, I think a trial judge could easily handle that by expediting matters. And this is what happens in a lot of the Federal trials. I would refer the Court to the 簫簫 to the brief of the Promise of Justice Initiative which shows that when we have joint trials, we get joint results. When we have severed trials, we get severed results. 25 for 25, joint trials, same result for the defendants. Woodson says we must have individualized sentencing at the penalty phase, so I do not see why the Zafiro test would not 簫簫 would 簫簫 would work for penalty phase proceedings without some sort of change. There has to be some recognition that 簫簫 that penalty phase proceedings are different than 簫簫 than all the other armed robbery, drug cases.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So how is that different from what the government said?

turns.name: JEFFREY T. GREEN
turns.text: I 簫簫 so I think we ought to lower the risk standard. We ought to lower the risk standard to a reasonable risk standard, not 簫簫 and that's what we propose in our brief at page 15. We should lower the standard because that would recognize the acute need for reliability and accuracy when it comes to penalty phase proceedings and the decision whether somebody is going to live or die.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: You said you were going to make a distinction between serious and reasonable earlier.

turns.name: JEFFREY T. GREEN
turns.text: Right. So a serious risk may be treated as a 簫簫 as a preponderance or a 簫簫 or above. With respect to reasonable, we're at a 簫簫 we're at a 簫簫 a 簫簫 maybe a likelihood. Maybe that's 簫簫 maybe that's perceived as 30 percent versus 55 percent or 60 percent, something like that. And indeed, this Court said in Boyde, with respect to reasonable likelihood, that is not a preponderance standard. If the Court has no further questions, we would urge 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. McAllister, five minutes.

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I will try to be brief. The only Eighth Amendment implication that this Court has recognized that really applies to this case is I go back to the Romano case where the Court said the admission of evidence that might or might not be in violation of State law is not an Eighth Amendment concern. The only time the Eighth 簫簫 Eighth Amendment comes into play is when the evidence is 簫簫 involves constitutionally protected conduct. An example would be Dawson v. Delaware, where they sought a capital sentence. He was a member of the Aryan Brotherhood in prison. It had nothing to do with an aggregating factor. That was First Amendment簫protected activity. The Court said that kind of thing could be an Eighth Amendment problem if you use it against the defendant. Their cases 簫簫 I could be wrong, but I think all of their cases, Zant, Stringer, the others that they refer to skewing the weighing process, are not about admission of evidence, they are about invalid aggravating factors. So you've basically told the jury there is something else, literally, on the scale, an aggravating factor that later the State says, no, that was 簫簫 that was not correct, that should not have been there. That's distinguishable from this case. I 簫簫 I agree completely with Justice Scalia's point, and the dissenting justice made it in the Kansas Supreme Court: If the evidence 簫簫 the corrupting influence evidence was so prejudicial to Reginald, why did Jonathan also get a death sentence? And I would close by saying that we all agree, I think, that the Constitution values accuracy and fairness. But it also values finality. And each of these individuals received an individualized sentence, presented all the evidence they wanted to. The jury was instructed to consider them individually. And if we were to undo this now, it would be very difficult for Kansas to go back. We'd be talking about having to redo all of the guilt phase evidence. And, again, you get into questions, separate proceedings, traumatizing victims yet again. None of that is necessary, because at the end of the day they got a fundamentally fair proceeding that gave each of them the sentence they deserved and the jury found warranted, both under the facts of this case and the law of Kansas. We would ask that you reverse the Kansas Supreme Court on this point.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. McAllister, I am sorry, but even if we do this and say that the sentencings didn't need to be severed, didn't the Kansas court hold on another ground that they were entitled to a new trial?

turns.name: STEPHEN R. MCALLISTER
turns.text: Yes. So that's the third question presented in the Petition, which my understanding is that is something the Court could reconsider. It's not denied. The Court granted Questions 1 and 3. The other question that we presented was the only other ground that the Kansas Supreme Court gave for reversal, that's a Confrontation Clause violation in terms of some of the hearsay evidence presented in the sentencing proceedings. So if the Court were to reverse on these, deny on that question, then the Kansas Supreme Court could, in fact, rely on that ground. But our hope would be that the Court would do something different than that if you reverse on these two questions. Unless there are further questions, thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The cases are submitted.
